content.problemStatement:
Chef has a new customer and he wants to prepare his order as soon as possible. While preparing, he sees that he is out of mint sauce. He has to run upstairs to get it from other kitchen. Chef is currently on the XthX^{th}Xth stair. He has to climb all the way up to the YthY^{th}Yth stair in minimum number of steps. In one step, Chef can do one of the following things:


Climb a single stair. ( i.e go from the XthX^{th}Xth stair to the (X+1)th(X+1)^{th}(X+1)th stair. )
Climb two stairs only if the final stair falls at a prime number position. ( i.e. go from the XthX^{th}Xth stair to the (X+2)th(X+2)^{th}(X+2)th stair, only if (X+2(X + 2(X+2) is a prime number)
Help Chef reach the YthY^{th}Yth stair from the XthX^{th}Xth stair in minimum number of steps.

See Explanation for more clarity.\textbf{See Explanation for more clarity.}See Explanation for more clarity.

**Note:** The input files are large. The use of Fast I/O is recommended.


Input Format

The first line contains an integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The TTT test cases then follow.
The first line of each test case contains XXX and YYY denoting the starting stair and ending stair respectively.


Output Format

Output a single integer representing minimum number of steps chef can take to reach from XthX^{th}Xth to YthY^{th}Yth stair.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two integers X and Y (1 ≤ X < Y ≤ 10^7) separated by a space, representing the starting and ending stair numbers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of steps required for Chef to reach stair Y from stair X.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Chef starts from 2nd2^{nd}2nd, goes to 3rd3^{rd}3rd stair, then to 5th5^{th}5th stair as 555 or (3+2)(3+2)(3+2) is prime number. Now, from 5th5^{th}5th stair, Chef goes to 7th7^{th}7th stair as 777 or (5+2)(5+2)(5+2) is a prime number, then Chef goes to 8th8^{th}8th stair, then to 9th9^{th}9th stair and finally to 10th10^{th}10th stair. This approach takes a total of 666 steps which is the minimum possible number of steps Chef can take to reach the 10th10^{th}10th stair starting from the 2nd2^{nd}2nd stair.
Test case 333: Starting from the 34th34^{th}34th stair, Chef moves through the stairs as following. 343434 → 353535 → 373737 → 383838 → 393939 → 414141 → 434343.

solution.approach:
Pre‑compute a boolean array isPrime[0…MAX] using the Sieve of Eratosthenes where MAX is the largest Y among all test cases. For each test case simulate the optimal greedy walk: start at position cur = X and steps = 0. While cur < Y, if cur+2 ≤ Y and isPrime[cur+2] is true, make a 2‑step jump (cur += 2); otherwise make a 1‑step move (cur += 1). Increment steps after each move. The greedy choice is optimal because a 2‑step jump never increases the total number of steps and skipping a possible 2‑step jump cannot lead to a better solution – any alternative path that avoids a feasible 2‑step jump is at best equal in length.
The total work per test case is O(Y‑X) which is fast enough after the O(MAX log log MAX) preprocessing. Memory usage is O(MAX) for the prime table.
solution.timeComplexity: Pre‑processing: O(MAX log log MAX) where MAX = max Y over all test cases. Per test case: O(Y‑X).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(MAX) for the prime boolean array.
